content.full_text:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 'Act').r Nothing in this letter should
be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken plage, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.2 The Fair political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it
renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppc ops. 7r.) Because you seek general
guidance rather than advice on a specific govemmental decision, we provide you with
informal assistance.' Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions
of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application, if arry, of other bodies ofiaw such as
common law conflicts of interest, or Government code Section 1090.
Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
luly 16,2007
Dustin Bloomfield
Treasurer, Board of Directors
Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District
P.O.Box244
Bethel Island, California 9451 1 -0244
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-07-091
Dear Mr. Bloomfield:
Please be advised that this letter is limited to the facts that you have provided to
us. In this letter we consider only your interest in your real property, andthe potential
conflicts of interests associated with your interest in that property. You have riot
disclosed any other economic interests. For example, if you have business interests that
arq ot have been, affected by a decision in a manner that has a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, you may not participate in the decision because you already
have a conflict of interest in that decision, based on a different subdivision of Section
87103. In addition, this advice is not intended to impact any issue which may be the
subject of a pending enforcement matter.
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle 2,Sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 The Com-ission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bXgXA),
copy enclosed.)
' Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 2
QUESTION
Are you prohibited from participatingin a governmental decision by the Bethel
Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created
water system?
CONCLUSION
You have a conflict of interest and may not participate in a goveflrmental decision
involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water
entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies.
FACTS
You are the Treasurer and a voting member of the Board of Directors for the
Bethel Island Municipal lmprovement District (BIMID). Bethel Island has
approximately 1,600 parcels of land. The uses of the land include agricultural,
commercial, and residential. There is no municipal or corporate water service on the
island. Most of the island is served by small private water companies, wells controlled
by mobile home park owners, or private wells.
A large developer has a tentative agreement with Diablo Water/ContraCosta
Water for service to a new development on the island that would include 490 single
family homes and 100 condominiums. The developer is now considering construction of
a high quality water treatment and distribution system that would include separate
irrigation water and fire hydrants for its project. The developer has "or-r*i"ated with a
number of existing small mutual water companies currentlyproviding water to nearby
properties to see if they wish to become part of the new water system.
The initial discussion with the small municipal water companies, along with the
project when built out, would serve approximately i0 percent of the populatejportion of
the island. The remaining2} percent of the populated portion of thelsland would not
have access to the new system. Those properties, apa.t fro* eighteen homes that receive
water from Diablo Water/Contra Costa Water, would continue to use untreated water
with high quantities of dissolved solids. You report that this could result in a negative
impact to the property values in the unserved areainrelation to the properties that receive
new services.
The developer has indicated a willingness to expand its water system to the
rernaining 20 percent of the island, or at least to make it possible for future expansion.
BIMID believes that the developer is ready to create a water system for 80 percent of the
island regardless of BIMID involvement, but if BIMID agrees to accept operational
control of the new water system, the developer would put in place equlpment that would
allow the entire island to be connected to the new water system.


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 3
In summary, as we understand i!, the decision in question , frdycome before
BIMID only after the developer has built or committeJto tuild i',i *iosystem
serving 80 percent of the island's residents, and the decision would be to determine if
BIMID will operate the system. The effeci of the a""iri"" t" have BIMID assume
;sil.':l:*'},",xfi: 
svstem wourd be the expansion of the svstem to serye the remaining
You own your own home, whic! I3qo"g the 2}percent of homes that would not
be receiving the new water system ifBIMID do.r"roir*Ipt operational control. you
would like to know whether you have a conflict of interesti nparticipating in a decision
to have BIMID assume control of the water system if the developer were to make that a
condition to expanding the new water system to the entire island.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, frle from bias causeJby their own financial interests
or the frnancial interest of persons who have .rpport"Jit em. (section sr001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or trer othciat poritio" io innu"r". a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in agoveflrmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable?trrut trr" govenrmental decision
will have amateial financial effect on.o_ne or more of the public official,s economic
interests' (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In ;;;.i" determine wrrether a public
official has a disqualifting conflict of interesi ir,_u grrr"n governmental decision, the
commission has adopted an eight-step analyticalfrramewirk. (RegulationlazooOXr>
(8)') The general rule, howev"i, i, thit aconflict of interest exists whenever a public
official Tuk". a govemmental decision that has a reaso"rti;;;;"""ui". *rrl*,
financial effect on one or more ofhis or her financial interests. (Section g7103.)
Step One: Are you a public Official?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Section
87100') Public officials include members or"o.rr-iit"es,"boards or commissions with
decision making authority. (Regulation 18701(a)(1).) 45 u *"mb., of the BIMID Board
of Directors, you are a public official under the'ect. '


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 4
step Two: will you Be Making, participating in Making, or using your
official Position to rnfluence a Governmental Decision?
A public offic_i{ "makes a govemmental decision" when the official , acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contlctual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.L) As a boardmember of
BIMID you will be making a goveflrmental decision if you vote on whether BIMID
should assume operational control of the water system.
In addition, a_public official "particrpates in making" a govemmental decision when
he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
tegarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Therefore, you will be participating in making
a govemmental decision if you advise, recommend or negotiate a course oiaction for
BIMID with regard to taking responsibility for the water system.a
Furthermore, a public official is using her or her official position to influence a
govemmental decision, before his or her agency, ifhe or she contacts, or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, or-consiritant of
BIMID regarding the decisions. (Regulationl87O2.3(a) Theiefore, you would be
influencing a governmental decision if you contact, appearbefore, oi Ltherwise attempt
to influence anymember, officer, ernployee, or cor.rliunt of BIMID regardm! the water
systern decision.
Step Three: What Are Your Economic Interests?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govenrmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a^iteiulfinancial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five "rr.r-"rat"d economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
o 
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 ormore (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)), or in which he or she is a director, officer, partnir,trustee,
empl9y9e, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
a Exception: M-akins olnarticipating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
govemmental function to represent himself or herself on mrtters ielated solely to the of{icial,s wholly
owned real property or business entity. (See Regulation lg7)2.4)


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 5
a
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
Indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Reguration
t8703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, incruding promised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87 1 03 (c); Regulation 187 03.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 1 03(e); Regulation 187 B.g;
o 
{ public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87 1 03 ; Regulation 1 3703.5.)
Assuming you own at least a $2,000 interest in your home, you have a
potentially disqualifying economic interest in your real property.
Every public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation iazO:.s.; However,
Regulation 18705.5 provides that when determining whether a decision would have a
material financial effect on an official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, a financial effect on the value of real property owned by the official will not be
considered. Because the only other economic interest you have indicated to us is your
interest in real property on the island, we will consider potential financial effects on that
property only, and will not further discuss potential effects on your personal finances or
other potential economic interests.
Step Four: fs Your Economic Interest Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?
Regulation 18704.2(6) states that apublic official has an economic interest that is
directly involved in a governmental decision if "the decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets , water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved seryices.,, As
we understand the facts, should BIMID decide to assume control of the new water
system, there would be improvements to water services to your property. This is because
BIMID's agreement to assume control over the new system would be apre-condition to
an expansion of the system to provide service to the 20 percent of the istand not covered
in the original build out and this 20 percent would include your property. Therefore, your
economic interest in your real property would be directly involved in this governmental
decision.


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 6
Step Five: Applicable Materiality Standard.
A conflictof interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
goveflrmental decision on a public official's economic interest is ,'material.,, (Section
87103; Regulation 1s700(a).) Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect
of a governmental decision on real property thatis directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material. Ulder the regulatiorl this presumptioimay be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeJble that the gorr"*-frrtal decision will
have any financial effect, even one penny's worth, on the real [roperty.
Step Six: fs it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financial Effect of the
Governmental Decision Upon Your Economic Interests WiII Be Material?
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means 'osubstantially likely.,, 6egutation 1g706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a Iertainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) Your account of the facts indicat"r thut if giMfO decides
not to assume control of the water system, properties outside the service area would
continue to use untreated water and could be adversely affected by that decision.
Accordingly, there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your real property.
Step Seven: The Public Generally Exception.
The "public generally exception" allows officials who may otherwise have a
disquali$ring interest to nonetheless participate in a govemmental decision if their
interest is affected in the same manner as the "public generally." Regulation lg717
provides:
"Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public officiai's economic interest
is material, a public official does not have a disqualifring conflict of
interest in the govemmental decision if the offici al canestablish that the
govemmental decision will affect the public official's economic interest in
a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the
decision willaffect the public generally as set forth in 2 Cal. Code Regs.
Sections 187 07 .1 -187 07 .9 ],
Regulation 18707.I provides the rule to determine if the material financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally. Under this rule, the financial effect oitfr" decision


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 7
on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if it will affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantialiy the same
manner" as it will affect the public official's economic interest.
For real property economic interests, the significant segment is defined under
Regulation 187 07 .1 (b)(l)(B) as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agerlcy or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agerrcy."
As you have explained, approximately 20 percentof the properties on the island
would be affected by BIMID's decision of whether or not to assume control of the new
water system, insofar as that decision would determine their access to the new water
system. Thus, it appears that a significant segment of the property owners or
homeowners on the island would be affected by this decision.
If the "significant segment" prong of this two-part test is met, it is next necessary
to determine if the significant segment is affected in substantially the same manner as the
public official's economic interest. The financial effect need not be identical for the
official's economic interest to be considered affected in "substantially the same manner.,,
(Regulation 187 07 .1 (bX2).)
The Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054, and,the Myers Advice Letter,
No. A-06-107, advised that the financial effect on real property within a "significant
segment" could varyby a specified finaflcial range equal to plus or minus two percent of
the value of the public official's property and still be considered to be affectedln
"substantially the same manner" as the official's property. For example, if an official,s
property is valued at $500,000, and the financial effect of the decision is a three-percent
increase ($15,000) on the official's property, increases between $5,000 and $2s,0oo
($10,000 Q%) plus or minus)) on properties within the significant segment would be
considered substantially the-same. As can be seen, these effects are measured in terms of
values and not percentages.s
Because all of the properties in the significant segment of the population will be
receiving the same new water services it is likely these properties wili either increase or
s For example, assume that the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision
results in a five-percent increase in properfy values for the city as a whole. The finaniial effect on a
$1,000,000 properly would be twice as much as the financial Lff"ct oo a $500,000 property, and would not
be considered substantially the same, even though the percentage change is the same. Conversely, a
financial effect of six-peJcent on a $500,000 property would be-substantially the same as a three-percent
increase on a $1,000,000 property, even though the percent increase is not tle same. (See Bergei, Id.)


File No. I-07-091
Page No. 8
decrease in value by a uniform percentage. However, because you have not provided us
with the relative property values of the properties affected, we are unable to determine
whether or not the "substantially same manner" test has been met. If you need further
assistance please write back and include those facts. Should you determine that the
homeowners in your significant segment are affected in substantially the same manner
then the public generally exception will apply and you would not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision.
Step Eight: Legally Required Participation
The final step of the conflicts analysis provides an exception for legally required
participation. At this point, we have no indication that this exception would be invoked
to enable BIMID to make this decision, andwe therefore end our analysis at this point.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Jarildl4LK-frtor*
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Le gal Division
SKBjsl
Enclosure

Sections 187 07 .1 -187 07 .9 ],
Regulation 18707.I provides the rule to determine if the material financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally. Under this rule, the financial effect oitfr" decision
on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if it will affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantialiy the same
manner" as it will affect the public official's economic interest.
For real property economic interests, the significant segment is defined under
Regulation 187 07 .1 (b)(l)(B) as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agerlcy or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agerrcy."
As you have explained, approximately 20 percentof the properties on the island
would be affected by BIMID's decision of whether or not to assume control of the new
water system, insofar as that decision would determine their access to the new water
system. Thus, it appears that a significant segment of the property owners or
homeowners on the island would be affected by this decision.
If the "significant segment" prong of this two-part test is met, it is next necessary
to determine if the significant segment is affected in substantially the same manner as the
public official's economic interest. The financial effect need not be identical for the
official's economic interest to be considered affected in "substantially the same manner.,,
(Regulation 187 07 .1 (bX2).)
The Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054, and,the Myers Advice Letter,
No. A-06-107, advised that the financial effect on real property within a "significant
segment" could varyby a specified finaflcial range equal to plus or minus two percent of
the value of the public official's property and still be considered to be affectedln
"substantially the same manner" as the official's property. For example, if an official,s
property is valued at $500,000, and the financial effect of the decision is a three-percent
increase ($15,000) on the official's property, increases between $5,000 and $2s,0oo
($10,000 Q%) plus or minus)) on properties within the significant segment would be
considered substantially the-same. As can be seen, these effects are measured in terms of
values and not percentages.s
Because all of the properties in the significant segment of the population will be
receiving the same new water services it is likely these properties wili either increase or
s For example, assume that the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision
results in a five-percent increase in properfy values for the city as a whole. The finaniial effect on a
$1,000,000 properly would be twice as much as the financial Lff"ct oo a $500,000 property, and would not
be considered substantially the same, even though the percentage change is the same. Conversely, a
financial effect of six-peJcent on a $500,000 property would be-substantially the same as a three-percent
increase on a $1,000,000 property, even though the percent increase is not tle same. (See Bergei, Id.)
decrease in value by a uniform percentage. However, because you have not provided us
with the relative property values of the properties affected, we are unable to determine
whether or not the "substantially same manner" test has been met. If you need further
assistance please write back and include those facts. Should you determine that the
homeowners in your significant segment are affected in substantially the same manner
then the public generally exception will apply and you would not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision.
Step Eight: Legally Required Participation
The final step of the conflicts analysis provides an exception for legally required
participation. At this point, we have no indication that this exception would be invoked
to enable BIMID to make this decision, andwe therefore end our analysis at this point.

QUESTION: Are you prohibited from participatingin a governmental decision by the Bethel
Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created
water system?

CONCLUSION: You have a conflict of interest and may not participate in a goveflrmental decision
involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water
entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies.
embedding.first_500_words: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 'Act').r Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken plage, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.2 The Fair political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppc ops. 7r.) Because you seek general guidance rather than advice on a specific govemmental decision, we provide you with informal assistance.' Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application, if arry, of other bodies ofiaw such as common law conflicts of interest, or Government code Section 1090. Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN 428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886 luly 16,2007 Dustin Bloomfield Treasurer, Board of Directors Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District P.O.Box244 Bethel Island, California 9451 1 -0244 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our FiIe No. I-07-091 Dear Mr. Bloomfield: Please be advised that this letter is limited to the facts that you have provided to us. In this letter we consider only your interest in your real property, andthe potential conflicts of interests associated with your interest in that property. You have riot disclosed any other economic interests. For example, if you have business interests that arq ot have been, affected by a decision in a manner that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, you may not participate in the decision because you already have a conflict of interest in that decision, based on a different subdivision of Section 87103. In addition, this advice is not intended to impact any issue which may be the subject of a pending enforcement matter. I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle 2,Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 The Com-ission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bXgXA), copy enclosed.) ' Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).) File No. I-07-091 Page No. 2 QUESTION Are you prohibited from participatingin a governmental decision by the Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created water system? CONCLUSION You have a conflict of interest and may not participate in a goveflrmental decision involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies. FACTS You are the Treasurer and a voting member of the Board of Directors for the Bethel Island Municipal lmprovement District (BIMID). Bethel Island has approximately 1,600 parcels of land. The uses of the land include agricultural, commercial, and residential. There is no municipal or corporate water service on the island. Most of the island is served by small private water companies, wells controlled by mobile
